Version 0.3 — Tentative Master Specification
Don Gunter — WodansEye Research Series — July 2026

0. Purpose
This document consolidates the current E-Tree architecture into one implementation and teaching specification.
It defines:

• the rooted project graph;

• the major capability stack;

• the meaning of T;

• the translation registry and Boost Graph planner;

• MakeT as the production engine;

• the persisted graph workbench as the required next bootstrap application;

• IML, p-code, simulation, stepping, debugging, and later LLVM lowering;

• file, network, device, and data infrastructure;

• distributed pumps and command-control services;

• agents and human team members;

• the path toward business logic, presentation, services, applications, and ERP;

• the lesson sequence used to refresh and guide the implementation team.
The architecture, phase boundaries, ownership of responsibilities, and acceptance gates are normative. Exact templates, storage layouts, GUI toolkit, ABI, and optimization thresholds remain provisional until reference implementations and conformance tests justify them.

1. Governing thesis
E-Tree is a graph-centered production and translation framework.
Its permanent center is:
typed information
+
declared translation capabilities
+
project and artifact dependencies
+
planning and policy
+
executable T implementationsThe root rule is:
Data and relations describe what exists and what is possible. A T performs work. A planner selects lawful work. MakeT builds the requested result.
The project is not one compiler, one CASE application, one GUI, one runtime, or one ERP product. It is a substrate from which those systems can be assembled.

2. Rooted project graph
The complete project is represented as one rooted typed graph with several views.
The root graph includes:
requirements
lessons
decisions
types
translations
models
files
artifacts
tests
tools
libraries
applications
people
agents
resources
risks
changes
milestones
releasesRepresentative relations include:
requires
teaches
implements
contains
translates_to
generates
tests
reviews
owns
consumes
produces
blocks
invalidates
supersedes
deploys
acceleratesNo single diagram shows the entire graph usefully. The system exposes typed projections.
2.1 Required graph views
Project and critical-path view
Shows lessons, tasks, resources, milestones, risks, dependencies, slack, and critical path.
Translation-capability view
Shows canonical information types as vertices and available translations as directed edges.
Artifact and build view
Shows files, generated artifacts, dependencies, stale state, caches, tests, and targets.
Semantic-model view
Shows classes, activities, states, components, data schemas, services, and domain models.
Control-flow view
Shows sequencing, branching, calls, loops, forks, joins, waits, faults, and returns.
Information-flow view
Shows typed values, tokens, records, streams, files, messages, references, and ownership moving between operations.
Execution-plan view
Shows selected T implementations, inserted adapters, pumps, queues, processes, workers, and JIT regions.
Deployment and resource view
Shows devices, processes, servers, workers, capabilities, locality, load, authorization, and communication paths.
Presentation view
Shows diagram coordinates, routing points, colors, grouping, visibility, and viewport state separately from semantic meaning.
These views are projections of one persisted graph system, not unrelated products.

3. Major capability stack
The stack is an architectural view over the rooted graph.
ERP and organizational production
↑
Agents and human–AI team workflows
↑
Services, tools, and applications
↑
Presentation for humans and AI
↑
Business logic and domain processes
↑
Data providers, repositories, clients, servers, and units of work
↑
Files, mappings, networks, devices, readers, writers, and adapters
↑
Translation registry, planning, generation, MakeT, and execution
↑
Mathematics, numerical contracts, p-code semantics, and exact references
↑
Hardware, ALU, registers, buses, memory, devices, and transportHigher layers may request lower-level capabilities. Lower layers return values, diagnostics, residuals, cost, and provenance upward.
The stack does not prohibit cross-layer optimization. It requires those optimizations to be explicit and contract-preserving.

4. Hardware and arithmetic floor
The baseline implementation assumes ordinary hardware:
CPU
ALU
register file
memory hierarchy
bus
threads
devices
networkAdvanced WodansEye mechanisms are registered as optional implementations when available:
Carry Ledger
carry-entropy-aware arithmetic
Register Switch Encoding
Bus Switch Encoding
run-aware operations
lane-granular allocation
deferred carry
cancellation routingThey are accelerators, not mandatory foundations.
Example:
IntegerAdd
├── reference scalar implementation
├── vectorized implementation
├── carry-ledger implementation
├── RSE/BSE implementation
└── JIT-specialized implementationThe planner selects among available implementations according to policy, guarantees, target hardware, and measured cost.
A reference path must remain available wherever practical.

5. Mathematics and numerical contracts
A mathematical or numerical translation declares more than input and output types.
A numeric contract may include:
representation
working precision
rounding policy
underflow policy
overflow policy
exception policy
reassociation permission
FMA/contraction permission
parallel-reduction policy
accuracy class
residual schema
condition estimate
stability class
reference backendPossible accuracy classes include:
exact
correctly rounded
faithfully rounded
stable approximation
bounded absolute error
bounded relative error
interval enclosed
residual certified
heuristic
unknownThe system must not assume two algebraically equivalent expressions are computationally interchangeable.
Stable numerical paths, exact-reference paths, p-code arithmetic, LLVM lowering, and optional hardware acceleration all obey the declared contract.

6. T: unit of work
T denotes a typed translation or operation.
T : INPUT_DOMAIN -> OUTPUT_DOMAINThe domains may be:
absent
single value
tuple
range
stream
cursor
record set
tree
graph
artifact
project
machine stateThe operation may be:
pure
stateful
effectful
local
remote
interpreted
generated
JIT compiled
distributedExamples:
Path -> ByteStream
ByteStream -> BlockStream
UTF8Text -> TokenStream
TokenStream -> CST
CST -> AST
AST -> TypedIR
TypedIR -> PCodeModule
TypedIR -> LLVMModule
Graph -> DOTDocument
Template + Context -> ExpandedDocument
Project + TargetRequest -> BuildReport
PCodeModule + State -> ExecutionResult6.1 What is not a T
The following are data or declarations, not work:
a value
a file
a graph vertex
a type
a relation edge
a model element
an artifactAn edge may reference a T implementation, but the edge itself states that a capability exists.
6.2 Cardinality
0 -> 1 or N generator
1 -> 1 map
N -> 1 reduction, merge, parser reduction
1 -> N expansion, broadcast
N -> M general transformation
N -> 0 validation, indexing, sink
0 -> 0 control or initialization6.3 Composition
A selected route or plan may be exposed as one composite T.
Composition must preserve or combine:
types
effects
state
fault behavior
cost
provenance
numeric contracts
activation requirementsThe production chain must be heterogeneous:
text -> tokens -> CST -> AST -> IR -> p-codeA chain limited to one repeated value type is tutorial scaffolding only.

7. Canonical type and translation registry
The registry is the permanent translation library.
7.1 Canonical type identity
Durable type identity must not depend solely on one process’s C++ RTTI.
A TypeId resolves a descriptor containing:
namespace
name
version
schema
representation
encoding
ownership/access formExamples:
etree.text.utf8@1
etree.dot.tokens@1
etree.dot.cst@1
etree.graph.semantic@1
etree.activity.model@1
etree.pcode.module@1
etree.llvm.module@1
data.csv.customer.v2
data.sql.postgresql.document.v17.2 Translation descriptor
A translation relation carries:
TranslationId
source TypeId
target TypeId
role
cardinality
effect
activation
requirements
guarantees
lossiness
determinism
streaming support
batch support
startup cost
per-item cost
memory estimate
energy estimate
quality class
provenance
implementation or factory referenceSeveral parallel edges may connect the same source and target types.
7.3 Direct index
(SourceTypeId, TargetTypeId) -> [TranslationId...]This supports fast lookup, generated registries, IDE completion, diagnostics, and agent tool discovery.
7.4 Boost Graph view
The same registry is exposed as a Boost Graph-compatible directed multigraph:
vertex property = TypeDescriptor
edge property = TranslationDescriptorBoost Graph provides the initial graph concepts and path algorithms. E-Tree owns the type model, storage adapters, policies, persistence, deterministic views, and later optimized graph representation.

8. Planning
A request contains:
source type
target type or target set
policy
available resources
required guarantees
current registry generation8.1 First planner
Dijkstra is the first planner for:
one source
one target
sequential directed path
nonnegative policy-selected costA policy may filter or weight edges according to:
losslessness
determinism
streaming
startup latency
runtime
memory
energy
precision
platform
security
availability8.2 General plans
Muxes, joins, forks, multi-target synthesis, shared prefixes, and parallel execution require a plan DAG or a later AND/OR or hypergraph planner.
Growth order:
linear shortest path
→ several targets
→ common-prefix reuse
→ explicit fork/join plans
→ resource-constrained planning
→ multi-objective planningThe system must not imply that one shortest-path call schedules every distributed workflow.
8.3 Materialization
Planning is not hot execution.
registry search
→ selected TranslationIds
→ inserted adapters
→ validated plan
→ executable composite T
→ optional source generation, p-code, or JIT
→ cached planCached plans are invalidated when the registry generation or relevant schemas change.

9. MakeT: production engine
MakeT translates project state and target requests into artifacts and reports.
MakeT:
ProjectInputs + TargetRequest
->
ArtifactSet + BuildReportIt contains or consults:
project graph
artifact dependency graph
translation registry
file catalog
symbol environment
macro environment
stale-artifact analysis
planner
scheduler
cache
diagnostics
provenance
worker resources9.1 Analysis
read project definitions
expand variables and macros
discover files and types
read metadata and headers
construct dependency graph
identify stale targets
find translation plans
merge shared work
choose execution policy
calculate parallel opportunities9.2 Synthesis
read and convert data
generate source
generate documentation
generate schemas
generate diagrams
generate tests
compile or JIT
run conformance checks
write artifacts
update manifests and cachesCompilation is one family of translation, not the definition of building.
9.3 Application integration
Applications submit target requests to MakeT.
They do not duplicate build, generation, translation discovery, or invalidation logic.

10. Persisted graph store
The first persistent semantic store uses explicit tables.
Vertex table
VertexId
GraphId
TypeId
Name
Flags
Ordinal
PropertySetId
CreatedRevision
DeletedRevisionEdge table
EdgeId
GraphId
SourceVertexId
TargetVertexId
RelationshipTypeId
Flags
Ordinal
PropertySetId
CreatedRevision
DeletedRevisionProperty table
PropertySetId
PropertyTypeId
ValueType
Value
OrdinalGraph table
GraphId
WorkspaceId
GraphTypeId
Name
SchemaId
Revision
RootVertexIdPresentation tables
DiagramView
VertexPresentation
EdgePresentation
ViewportStateSemantic equality does not depend on position, color, routing points, collapse state, or zoom.
Graph changes
Edits are typed GraphDelta commands:
